title: Prophet Hosea, St. Zadok the Priest, The 128 Persian Martyrs & St. Porphyrius the Actor
primary_saint: The Prophet Hosea, St. Zadok the Priest and Martyr, the One Hundred and Twenty-Eight Persian Martyrs, and St. Porphyrius the Actor
narrative:
On the twenty-sixth day of Yekatit, the holy Church gathers four witnesses under the sign of the prophet Hosea's great theme: that the love of God for His people is not contingent upon their fidelity, that it pursues the beloved through apostasy and exile and degradation, and that its final word is always return, restoration, and the renewal of the covenant. The Prophet Hosea stands first, that most personally costly of all the prophets, whose marriage to an unfaithful wife was itself the prophetic act — the living parable through which God communicated the dynamics of divine love to a people who could not hear the abstract doctrine. Hosea's willingness to redeem Gomer from her degradation, to buy her back and bring her home, is the Old Testament's most searingly human image of what God does with the wayward people He refuses to abandon, and the Ethiopian tradition reads this story as a direct prefiguration of the Incarnation, in which God came Himself to buy back the humanity that had sold itself into bondage.

Saint Zadok the Priest is this day's figure of priestly suffering — a confessor and martyr of the Persian church who was put to death during the persecutions of Shapur II, along with members of his household and community. Zadok's name connects him symbolically to the ancient high-priestly line of Israel, and the Ethiopian tradition honours him as a representative of the long succession of priestly witnesses who have sustained the Church's sacramental life at the cost of their own. The priest who celebrates the Eucharist in defiance of an imperial prohibition is performing an act that every age of persecution has required, and Zadok performed it until the soldiers came.

The One Hundred and Twenty-Eight Persian Martyrs are the great company of this day's commemoration — another wave from the vast Persian martyrdom that claimed so many thousands of Christians during the fourth century. Their number, one hundred and twenty-eight, is preserved with the precision the tradition reserves for groups large enough to seem incalculable but for whom the Church insists on keeping count, refusing to let them dissolve into an undifferentiated mass. Each of the hundred and twenty-eight had a name, a family, a community, a reason for being in the wrong place at the wrong time from the Persian state's perspective; the Synaxarium's preservation of their number is the minimum act of justice that memory can perform on behalf of those whose individual stories the record cannot hold.

Saint Porphyrius the Actor brings this assembly its most unexpected figure: a man of the theatre commissioned to perform a public parody of Christian baptism before a crowd at Caesarea. Like Gelasius the Actor on the fourth day of this month, Porphyrius found himself transformed at the very moment of the performance: descending into the water for the mock baptism, he rose from it a genuine Christian and proclaimed his faith to the astonished audience. He was brought before the emperor, who ordered his execution, and he died still wet from the water in which grace had overtaken him. The Ethiopian tradition places Porphyrius alongside Hosea precisely because his conversion enacts the prophet's theme: God does not wait for the beloved to come to Him under respectable circumstances. He meets the actor in the baptismal pool, the prophet in the degraded marriage, the priest in the prison, the martyrs in the courtyard of execution. Wherever humanity is found, there the pursuing love of God has already arrived. Amen.
